---
title: "Kernel and image"
description: "Kernel and image of a linear map, the rank–nullity theorem, and a first look at linear functionals — an augmented summary of Axler's LADR §3.B."
date: "2026-06-05"
categories: ["mathematics", "linear-algebra", "ladr"]
execute:
    echo: true
---

{{< include ../../_macros.qmd >}}

This is an augmented summary of Section 3.B of [Linear Algebra Done Right](https://linear.axler.net/index.html) by Sheldon Axler.

## Kernel and image
Suppose that $T:V\to W$ is a linear map. Then you can obtain a subspace of $V$ and a subspace of $W$ out of $T$, called the *kernel* and the *image* of the map respectively.

#### Kernel
We saw that $T$ must map $0$ to $0$. But it may send other vectors in $V$ to $0$ as well. The set of all $v\in V$ with $Tv=0$ is called the **kernel** (or the **null space**) of $T$, and is written as $\ker T$. It is easy to check that $\ker T$ is a subspace of $V$. (For starters, we already saw that $0\in\ker T$.)

:::{#exm-}
The kernel of the differentiation linear map $D:\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})\to\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ consists of the constant polynomials. 
:::

###### Injectivity and kernel
A linear map is injective (maps different inputs to different outputs) if and only if its kernel is trivial.

:::{.proof}
Suppose that a linear map $T$ is injective. Then for any $v\in V$ such that $Tv=0$, we have

$$Tv=T0=0\implies v=0.$$

So the kernel of $T$ is just $\{0\}$.

Conversely, suppose that $\ker T=\{0\}$. If we have $Tv_1=Tv_2$, then this implies 

$$T(v_1-v_2)=0\implies v_1-v_2\in\ker T\implies v_1-v_2=0\implies v_1=v_2.$$

Therefore, $T$ is injective.
:::

:::{#exm-linind}
Let $v_1,\cdots,v_n\in V$, and define $T\in\mathcal{L}(\mb{F}^n,V)$ as

$$T(z_1,\cdots,z_n)=z_1v_1+\cdots+z_nv_n.$$

Then the linear independence of the $v_i$s is equivalent to the triviality of $\ker T$, and so it occurs if and only if $T$ is injective.
:::

###### Kernel complement
Suppose that $V$ is finite-dimensional and $T\in\mathcal{L}(V,W)$. Let $U$ be a complement of $\ker T$, and let $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ and $v'_1,\cdots,v'_m$ be bases of $\ker T$ and $U$ respectively. We know that $\ker T\cap U=\{0\}$ as they are complements. 

Let $v$ be an arbitrary vector in $V$ with

$$v=a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n+a'_1v'_1+\cdots+a'_mv'_m.$$

Then we have

$$Tv=T(a'_1v'_1+\cdots+a'_mv'_m),$$

where $a'_1v'_1+\cdots+a'_mv'_m$ can cover any vector in $U$. Therefore, $\im\ T$ (see below) coincides with $\{Tu:u\in U\}$. By the rank-nullity theorem (see below), we have

$$\dim\im\ T=\dim V-\dim\ker T=m.$$

Since $Tv'_1,\cdots,Tv'_m$ is a spanning set of $\im\ T$ with the right size, it is a basis of $\im\ T$.

#### Image
The **image**^[Note that we also use the term "image" to refer to the individual $Tv$.] (or the **range**) of $T$ is the set of all $w\in W$ that $T$ sends the vectors in $V$ to:

$$\im\ T=\{Tv:v\in V\}.$$

By the closure of $V$, $\im\ T$ is also closed under addition and multiplication, as we can check. In addition, the flip side of $T0=0$ is that $0\in\im\ T$. Therefore, the image is a subspace of $W$.

:::{#exm-}
The image of the differentiation linear map $D:\mathcal{P}_5(\mathbb{R})\to\mathcal{P}_5(\mathbb{R})$ is $\mathcal{P}_4(\mathbb{R})$.
:::

###### Surjectivity and kernel
By definition, a linear map $T:V\to W$ is surjective if and only if $\im\ T=W$.

:::{#exm-}
Consider @exm-linind. The $v_i$s span $V$ if and only if $T$ is surjective.
:::

###### Image of spanning set
Suppose that $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ spans $V$. Clearly, $Tv_1,\cdots,Tv_n$ does not span $W$ in general. A trivial example is when $T$ is a zero map. But it does span $\im\ T\subseteq W$.

:::{.proof}
Suppose that $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ spans $V$, and consider an arbitrary $Tv\in\im\ T$ where $v=a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n$. Then we have

$$Tv=T(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)=a_1Tv_1+\cdots+a_nTv_n.$$

Therefore, the $Tv_i$s span $\im\ T$.
:::

#### More on injectivity and surjectivity
###### Injectivity and product
If $S_1,\cdots,S_n$ are injective linear maps (with valid product), then it is easy to see that $S_1\cdots S_n$ is also injective.

###### Injectivity and linear independence
Previously, we have seen that linear maps do not preserve linear independence. But an *injective* linear map does preserve it. 

:::{.proof}
Suppose that $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ is linearly independent. If we have

$$0=a_1Tv_1+\cdots+a_nTv_n=T(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n),$$

then $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n\in\ker T$. Since $\ker T=\{0\}$ for injections, this linear combination is zero. By the linear independence of $v_1,\cdots,v_n$, we have $a_i=0$. Therefore, the $Tv_i$s are linearly independent.
:::

###### Set of injective/surjective maps
The set of injective maps in $\mathcal{L}(V, W)$ does not form a subspace if $\dim V>0$, for then the zero map is not injective.

The set of surjective maps does not form a subspace if $\dim W>0$, for then the zero map is not surjective.

###### Set of non-injective maps
Suppose that $\dim W\geq\dim V\geq 2$. The map of all non-injective maps in $\mathcal{L}(V, W)$ does not form a subspace. (Note that this does not hold in some fields outside $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{C}$.)

:::{.proof}
Let $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ and $w_1,\cdots,w_m$ be bases of $V$ and $W$ respectively. Consider the linear maps $T,S:V\to W$ specified as

$$
Tv_i=
\begin{cases}
0&\text{if }i=2\\
w_i&\text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\quad\text{and}\quad
Sv_i=
\begin{cases}
0&\text{if }i=1\\
w_i&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
$$

Neither are injective. Now suppose there are two vectors

$$
v=a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n
\quad\text{and}\quad
v'=a'_1v_1+\cdots+a'_nv_n
$$

such that

$$(T+S)v=(T+S)v'.$$

This equality implies

$$
a_1w_1+a_2w_2+\sum_{i=3}^n2a_iw_i=a'_1w_1+a'_2w_2+\sum_{i=3}^n2a'_iw_i,
$$

and the linear independence of the $w_i$s means that $a_i=a'_i$. Therefore, we have $v=v'$ and so $S+T$ is injective. This means that the set of all non-injective maps is not closed under addition, and cannot be a subspace.
:::

###### Set of non-surjective maps
Suppose that $\dim V\geq\dim W\geq 2$. (Note the difference from the previous case.) The set of all non-surjective maps in $\mathcal{L}(V, W)$ does not form a subspace.

:::{.proof}
Let $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ and $w_1,\cdots,w_m$ be bases of $V$ and $W$ respectively. Consider the linear maps $T,S:V\to W$ specified as

$$
Tv_i=
\begin{cases}
w_i&\text{if }i<m\\
0&\text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\quad\text{and}\quad
Sv_i=
\begin{cases}
w_i&\text{if }i=m\\
0&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
$$

Neither are surjective. But for every $w\in W$ with

$$w=a_1w_1+\cdots+a_mw_m,$$

we have

$$(T+S)(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_mv_m)=w,$$

and so $T+S$ is surjective. The set of all non-surjective maps is not closed under addition, and cannot be a subspace.
:::

#### Finite kernel and image implies finite domain
Suppose that a vector space $V$ has a linear map $T:V\to W$ whose kernel and image are finite-dimensional. Then $V$ is itself finite-dimensional.

:::{.proof}
Let $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ and $w_1,\cdots,w_m$ be bases of $\ker T$ and $\im\ T$ respectively. Let $v'_1,\cdots,v'_m$ be such that $Tv'_i=w_i$, which exist because $w_i\in\im\ T$.

For contradiction, suppose that the set of vectors $v_1,\cdots,v_n,v'_1,\cdots,v'_m$ does not span $V$. That means that there exists $v\in V$ where no scalars satisfy

$$v=a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n+b_1v'_1+\cdots+b_mv'_m.$$

So there are no $b_i$s such that

$$Tv=b_1w_1+\cdots+b_mw_m,$$

which contradicts the assumption that $w_1,\cdots,w_m$ is a basis of $\im\ T$ (and so spans $\im\ T$). Therefore, the set does span $V$, and so $V$ is finite-dimensional.
:::

#### Chain complex
Suppose that $S,T\in\mathcal{L}(V,V)$ with $\im\ S\subset\ker T$. Then we have $TS=0$. This is the defining relation of a **chain complex**, which is important in more advanced topics.

## Rank-nullity theorem
The dimension of $\ker T$ is called the **nullity** of $T$, while that of $\im\ T$ is called its **rank**. As it turns out, these add up to the dimension of $V$ when $V$ is finite-dimensional.

:::{.proof}
Let $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ be a basis of $\ker T\subseteq V$, and suppose that $v'_1,\cdots,v'_m$ extends this set to form a basis of $V$. Since 

$$\dim V=n+m=\dim\ker T+m,$$

we need to show that $\dim\im\ T=m$. This can be achieved by proving that $Tv'_1,\cdots,Tv'_m$ is a basis of $\im\ T$.

Note that the span of $v'_1,\cdots,v'_m$ is the kernel complement, and so 

$$\im\ T=\{T(a_1v'_1+\cdots+a_mv'_m):a_i\in\mb{F}\}=\text{span}(Tv'_1,\cdots,Tv'_m).$$

That is, $Tv'_1,\cdots,Tv'_m$ spans $\im\ T$. What about linear independence? Suppose that

$$a'_1Tv'_1+\cdots+a'_mTv'_m=0.$$

After pulling out $T$, we see that this implies $a'_1v'_1+\cdots+a'_mv'_m\in\ker T$. In other words,

$$a'_1v'_1+\cdots+a'_mv'_m=a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n$$

for some choice of the $a_i$s. Recall that $v_1,\cdots,v_n,v'_1,\cdots,v'_m$ is linearly independent because it is a basis. So moving everything to one side reveals that $a_i=a'_i=0$. Therefore, the $Tv'_i$s are linearly independent.

So we have shown that $Tv'_1,\cdots,Tv'_m$ is a basis of $\im\ T$, leading to

$$\dim V=\dim\ker T+\dim\im\ T.$$

:::

:::{#exm-}
There is no linear map $T:\mathbb{R}^5\to\mathbb{R}^5$ with $\im\ T=\ker T$, because that requires 

$$5=\dim\ker T+\dim\im\ T=2\dim\ker T.$$

:::

:::{#exm-}
If a linear map $T$ maps from $\mathbb{R}^8$ to $\mathbb{R}^3$ and $\ker T$ has 5 dimensions, then we immediately know that $T$ is surjective because

$$\dim\im\ T=\dim \mathbb{R}^8-\dim\ker T=8-5=3$$

:::

#### Kernel and image of linear map products
###### Kernel
Suppose that $U$ and $V$ have finite dimensions, and let $T:U\to V$ and $S:V\to W$ be linear maps. Then the nullity of the product $ST$ is related to those of $S$ and $T$ as

$$\dim\ker ST\leq\dim\ker S+\dim\ker T.$$

:::{.proof}
There are two ways for $u\in U$ to lie in the kernel of $ST$. Either $u$ is directly in the kernel of $T$, or otherwise $Tu$ is in the kernel of $S$. This shows two facts hold: first, $\ker T$ is a subset of $\ker ST$, and second, $T$ maps each vector of $\ker ST$ to a vector of $\ker S$.

Therefore, let us consider the restriction $T|_{\ker ST}:\ker ST\to\im\ T$. The rank-nullity theorem then gives us

$$
\dim\ker ST=\dim\ker T|_{\ker ST}+\dim\im\ T|_{\ker ST}.
$$

By the first fact, we find $\dim\ker T|_{\ker ST}=\dim\ker T$, while the second fact shows that $\im\ T|_{\ker ST}\subseteq\ker S$. Therefore, we have

$$
\dim\ker ST\leq\dim\ker S+\dim\ker T
$$

as required.
:::

###### Image
Let the setup be the same as above. The rank of $ST$ is related to those of $S$ and $T$ as

$$\dim\im\ ST\leq\min(\dim\im\ S,\dim\im\ T).$$

:::{.proof}
First of all, we should note that $x\leq\min(y,z)$ is equivalent to $x\leq y$ and $x\leq z$.

Since we have

$$\im\ ST=\{S(Tu):Tu\in\im\ T\},$$

it is easy to see that $\im\ ST\subseteq\im\ S$ and so

$$\dim\im\ ST\leq\dim\im\ S.$$

It is also not too surprising that $\dim\im\ ST$ is no greater than $\dim\im\ T$, for intuitively speaking, acting $S$ on $\im\ T$ cannot *enlarge* this space. If we restrict the domain of $S$ to $\im\ T$, we restrict its image to $\im\ ST$, as we see from the definition of $\im\ ST$. Therefore, $S|_{\im\ T}:\im\ T\to\im\ ST$ is a surjection, and the rank-nullity theorem gives us

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dim\im\ T&=\dim\ker S|_{\im\ T}+\dim\im\ ST\\
&\geq \dim\im\ ST.
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, we have 

$$\dim\im\ ST\leq\min(\dim\im\ S,\dim\im\ T)$$

as required.
:::

#### Injection and surjection
###### Injection and dimension
Let $V$ and $W$ be finite-dimensional. An injection $T:V\to W$ exists if and only if $\dim V\leq\dim W$.

:::{.proof}
For contradiction, suppose that $\dim V>\dim W$. Then the rank-nullity theorem leads to

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dim\ker T&=\dim V-\dim\im\ T\\
&\geq\dim V-\dim W\\
&>0.
\end{aligned}
$$

But $\ker T=\{0\}$ when $T$ is an injection. So if $T$ is an injection, then $\dim V\leq\dim W$.

Conversely, suppose that $\dim V\leq\dim W$, and let $w_1,\cdots,w_{\dim V+k}$ be a basis of $W$. Let $T$ be a linear map specified by $Tv_i=w_i$ for $i=1,\cdots,\dim V$. Clearly, we have $\ker T=\{0\}$, and so this is an injection.
:::

###### Surjection and dimension
Let $V$ and $W$ be finite-dimensional. A surjection $T:V\to W$ exists if and only if $\dim V\geq \dim W$.

:::{.proof}
Suppose that a surjection $T:V\to W$ exists, whose image is $W$ by the definition of surjection. Then the rank-nullity theorem leads to

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dim V&=\dim\ker T+\dim\im\ T\\
&=\dim\ker T+\dim W\\
&\geq\dim W.
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversely, suppose that $\dim V\geq\dim W$, and let $w_1,\cdots,w_{\dim W}$ be a basis of $W$. Then the linear map defined by

$$
Tv_i=
\begin{cases}
w_i&\text{if }i\leq\dim W\\
0&\text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

is clearly a surjection. 
:::

#### Linear equations
Consider a system of $m$ linear equations in $n$ variables

$$
\begin{gather}
a_{1,1}x_1+\cdots+a_{1,n}x_n=c_1\\
\vdots\\
a_{m,1}x_1+\cdots+a_{m,n}x_n=c_m,
\end{gather}
$$

where $a_{i,j},x_i\in\mb{F}$. If we define a linear map $T:\mb{F}^n\to\mb{F}^m$ as

$$T(x_1,\cdots,x_n)=(\sum_{i=1}^na_{1,i}x_i,\cdots,\sum_{i=1}^na_{m,i}x_i),$$

then this system can be represented as

$$T(x_1,\cdots,x_n)=(c_1,\cdots,c_m).$$

###### Homogeneous system
If $c_i=0$, then we have a **homogeneous** system. We often ask whether there are solutions to this system other than the trivial one $x_i=0$. In the linear map language, there exist non-trivial solutions when $\ker T\neq\{0\}$, in other words when $T$ is non-injective. We saw that it is always non-injective when $n>m$, i.e. when there are more variables than equations.

###### Inhomogeneous system
On the other hand, we may ask if there exist a combination of the $c_i$s to which no solutions exist. In the linear map language, no solutions exist when $T$ is non-surjective. We have seen that it is always non-surjective when $n<m$, i.e. when there are more equations than variables.

## Some early results on linear functionals
#### As coordinates of linear map
For a linear map $T:V\to W$ and a basis $w_1,\cdots,w_n$ of $\im\ T$, there exist linear functionals $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n:V\to\mb{F}$ such that the $i$th coordinate of $Tv$ is given by $\phi_i(v)$.

:::{.proof}
Since the linear combination

$$Tv=a_1w_1+\cdots+a_nw_n$$

is unique for a given basis, the function $\phi_i:v\mapsto a_i$ is well-defined for each $i$. As

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(v_1+v_2)&=Tv_1+Tv_2\\
&=\phi_1(v_1)w_1+\cdots+\phi_n(v_1)w_n+\phi_1(v_2)w_1+\cdots+\phi_n(v_2)w_n\\
&=(\phi_1(v_1)+\phi_1(v_2))w_1+\cdots+(\phi_n(v_1)+\phi_n(v_2))w_n,
\end{aligned}
$$

we have $\phi_i(v_1+v_2)=\phi_i(v_1)+\phi_i(v_2)$ and so $\phi_i$ is additive. We can similarly show that $\phi_i$ is homogeneous.
:::

#### Kernel hyperplanes
The kernel of a linear functional $\phi:V\to\mb{F}$ partitions $V$ into its translated copies. That is, for $u\notin\ker\phi$, we have the direct sum

$$V=\ker\phi\oplus\{au:a\in\mb{F}\}.$$

:::{.proof}
We first need to show that, for each $v\in V$, there exist $w\in\ker\phi$ and $a\in\mb{F}$ such that

$$v=w+au.$$

If this expression did hold, we would have

$$\phi(v)=\phi(w+au)=a\phi(u)\implies a=\frac{\phi(v)}{\phi(u)},$$

where we recall that $\phi$ maps to $\mb{F}$. So by setting

$$
a=\frac{\phi(v)}{\phi(u)}\quad\text{and}\quad w=v-\frac{\phi(v)}{\phi(u)}u\in\ker\phi,
$$

we obtain the desired decomposition. Therefore, we have $V=\ker\phi+\{au:a\in\mb{F}\}$. 

Now, let us check that the sum is direct. If $au\in\ker \phi$ for any $a\neq0$, we would have

$$\phi(au)=a\phi(u)=0\implies \phi(u)=0,$$

contradicting $u\notin\ker \phi$. Hence, we find that

$$\ker \phi\cap\{au:a\in\mb{F}\}=\{0\}$$

and the sum is direct.
:::

###### Linear functionals sharing same kernel

As a corollary, consider $\phi_1,\phi_2:V\to\mb{F}$ that share the same kernel $K$. Then we have $\phi_1=c\phi_2$ for some $c\in\mb{F}$. That is, the kernel uniquely determines a linear functional up to a multiplicative constant.

:::{.proof}
We need to show that there exists a single $c\in\mb{F}$ such that

$$\phi_1(v)=c\phi_2(v)$$

for all $v\in V$. Let us choose some $u\notin K$. Then for any $v\in V$, we have

$$
v=k+\frac{\phi_2(v)}{\phi_2(u)}u
$$

for some $k\in K$, as we have seen previously. Subsequently, we find

$$\phi_1(v)=\phi_1(k+\frac{\phi_2(v)}{\phi_2(u)}u)=\frac{\phi_2(v)}{\phi_2(u)}\phi_1(u)=\frac{\phi_1(u)}{\phi_2(u)}\phi_2(v).$$

As $v$ was arbitrary, we see that $\phi_1(u)/\phi_2(u)$ is the sought constant $c$.
:::
